transcript: [doctor] patient mrn abc 123 . date of birth january 1 , 1960 . patient is a 60-year-old right handed male referred today for evaluation of numbness and tingling . hello mr. cook , how are you doing this morning ? [patient] hi dr. martinez , this weekend was a bit rough . i've had this numbness and tingling for a few weeks now . [doctor] so it's in both of your hands ? [patient] yes , ever since my car wreck last december when i broke them , well once they healed i mean i noticed it more , the tingling and the numbness , [doctor] and you still wear the splint only on the right wrist ? [patient] no , i also wear the left sometimes , the right is just worse but the splint helps . both have really gotten worse over the last month and i broke them my right wrist was definitely in worse shape . i've also been having some mild stiffness in my neck too . i hit my head during the accident and they told me it was a contusion . [doctor] and where would you say the tingling and numbness is ? [patient] on this side , it goes from here and here up to about here on the other side , it's from here to here . [doctor] i see . so on the right side you're pointing from the middle and ring finger all the way up the forearm and on the left the symptoms extend from the pinky and ring finger and is there any pain or just tingling and numbness ? it's mainly just sore , sometimes they throb but overall they feel weaker too especially when i'm using them . the tingling happens more when they hurt , but when it goes away , the tingling is dull . [patient] and when do you feel these symptoms ? well , really i guess just more so at night and they come and go throughout the day . [doctor] okay and do you experience any loss of sensation in your hands or fingers ? [patient] like feeling something , no , not that i can really think of though in general they maybe less sensitive than they should be but i can still feel they also gave me some nerve pain medicine , but i have n't been taking it like i should . [doctor] and are you still on the coreg for hypertension and the lexapro for anxiety ? [patient] yes , i'm still taking those . i had prediabetes too , but i got it under control by changing my diet . [doctor] and no history of thyroid issues , correct ? [patient] no . [doctor] ri thank you mr. cook okay first we'll go over your review of systems correct me if i'm wrong in any of this constitutional you have fatigue and weight gain gi you have excessive appetite and bloating eyes have floaters , cardiovascular shows shortness of breath and leg swelling , endocrine , you're always hungry , skin , you have some nailbed changes , dryness , and itching , musculoskeletal , you have gout and muscle pain , and the remainder of the review of systems is negative . [patient] yeah , that's all correct . [doctor] alright we are going to go ahead and do the neurologic examination . let's look at the right hand first . can you feel this ? [patient] yeah , but a little less on these two fingers . [doctor] sensation is normal except pinprick sensation diminished in the third and fourth fingers on the right and how about on the left ? [patient] yeah but these two are little less . [doctor] sensation is decreased in the fourth and fifth fingers on the left . okay , let's test your reflexes now . reflexes are 1+ and symmetric . 2+ knees , toes are downgoing bilaterally . alright , i'm going to tap on your right wrist . do you feel anything ? [patient] yeah , it feels like a zing of my wrist and something into my elbow too . [doctor] and how about the left side ? [patient] yeah , same on that side too . [doctor] tinel's sign positive at both wrists and both elbows . rest of exam is normal . alright so what i am seeing today is bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome or potentially this could also be bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome . however , your symptom presentation suggests bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome as the nerves in your wrist are aggravated by your past trauma . this is causing the pain and tingling you were describing to me . okay . so how we make it better ? first , i want to determine exactly where the nerve irritation is coming from . we will need to schedule you for a nerve test , which is called an emg test . they will measure the responsiveness of your nerves to truly pinpoint where you're having your issues . [patient] that sounds fine to me . [doctor] great . after the tests are complete , you can return here for a follow-up visit . if you're still having symptoms , we can consider a referral to our hand clinic possibly for injections or carpal tunnel release surgery . you could also refer , we could also refer you to an orthopedist for the same treatment . [patient] sounds good document . [doctor] alright keep wearing your splints and i will see you after those studies . [patient] thank you .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Numbness and tingling of both hands.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Elijah Cook is a 60-year-old male referred today for evaluation of numbness and tingling of both hands.

The patient complains of numbness, tingling, and pain in both hands which began in 12/2019 after he broke both wrists in a motor vehicle accident and which has progressively worsened since then. He continues to wear a splint on the right wrist all the time and on the left only intermittently, which he states is because the right wrist was injured more severely in the accident. The sensation radiates from the hands into the forearms and is equal bilaterally. The 4th and 5th fingers in the left hand and 3rd and 4th on the right-hand tingle. He states that his fingers are sore with palpation and seem weaker than before. Any exertion exacerbates the tingling, and after something exacerbates the pain, the tingling seems "dull". The symptoms come and go and seem to occur mostly at night. He denies complete loss of sensation in any of his fingers, but states that they seem less sensitive than they should be. He reports mild stiffness in his neck. He notes that he did hit his head during the car accident and was diagnosed with a contusion. He states that he has been given "nerve pain medicine" but has not been taking it.

He currently takes Coreg for his hypertension and Lexapro for his anxiety. He reports a history of prediabetes, which he has brought under control via dietary changes. He denies any history of thyroid issues.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

 Constitutional Symptoms: Easily fatigued, weight gain.
 Eyes: Floaters.
 Cardiovascular: Bilateral peripheral edema.
 Respiratory: Shortness of breath.
 Gastrointestinal: Excessive appetite, abdominal bloating.
 Musculoskeletal: Gout, muscle pain.
 Integumentary: Dry itchy skin, nail bed changes.
 Endocrine: Excessive hunger.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neurological
Pinprick sensation intact in the 3rd and 4th fingers on the right hand and 4th and 5th fingers on the left hand. Reflexes are 1+ and symmetric, 2+ knees. Toes down going bilaterally. Tinel's sign positive at both wrists and elbows.

ASSESSMENT

 Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome
Mr. Cook is a 60-year-old male referred today for evaluation of numbness and tingling of both hands. His symptom presentation and exam findings today suggest this is bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome or, potentially, this could also be bilateral cubital tunnel syndrome.

PLAN

Diagnosis discussed with patient, I explained that the nerves in his wrists are aggravated by the past trauma causing the pain and tingling. Further testing is indicated to measure the responsiveness of the nerves to determine exactly where the nerve irritation is coming from. We will plan for a follow-up visit after the EMG testing is performed. If still symptomatic, we can consider a referral to our Hand Clinic possibly for injections or carpal tunnel release surgery. We could also refer to an orthopedist for the same treatment.

INSTRUCTIONS

Follow-up after EMG testing.
notes:
Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 60-year-old right-handed male, presents with a 2-week history of numbness and tingling in both hands, which started after a car accident in December where he broke both wrists. He reports worsening symptoms over the last month, with increased numbness and tingling, particularly at night, and occasional pain or throbbing sensations in his hands. He also experiences mild stiffness in his neck. The patient denies any loss of sensation in his hands or fingers, but notes that they may be less sensitive than usual.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of hypertension, treated with Coreg, and anxiety, treated with Lexapro. He has also had prediabetes, which he controlled through dietary changes. He denies any history of thyroid issues.

Review of systems: The patient reports fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, hunger, nailbed changes, dryness, and itching. He also has a history of gout and muscle pain.

Current medications: Coreg for hypertension, Lexapro for anxiety.

Objective:

Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The patient's sensation is normal in the right hand, except for diminished pinprick sensation in the third and fourth fingers. The sensation is decreased in the fourth and fifth fingers on the left. Reflexes are 1+ and symmetric. Tinel's sign is positive at both wrists and elbows.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient has bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, potentially caused by nerve irritation from past trauma.

Problem list: Carpal tunnel syndrome; cubital tunnel syndrome; prediabetes; hypertension; anxiety; gout; muscle pain.

Plan:

I prescribed a nerve test, an EMG, to determine the exact location of the nerve irritation. I instructed the patient to continue wearing his splints and to return for a follow-up visit after the tests are complete. If symptoms persist, we will consider a referral to the hand clinic for injections or carpal tunnel release surgery, or to an orthopedist for further treatment.

Follow-up: The patient will return for a follow-up visit after the EMG test results are available.

Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 60-year-old right-handed male, presents with numbness and tingling in both hands, which started a few weeks after a car accident in December where he broke his wrists. He reports that the symptoms have worsened over the last month. He also experiences mild stiffness in his neck and has a history of a head contusion from the accident. The patient describes the numbness and tingling as starting from the middle and ring finger on the right hand and from the pinky and ring finger on the left hand, extending up to the forearm. He reports that the symptoms are mainly sore, with occasional throbbing and weakness, particularly when using his hands. The tingling is more pronounced when his hands hurt, and he describes it as dull when it subsides. The symptoms are more noticeable at night and come and go throughout the day.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of hypertension, for which he is taking Coreg, and anxiety, for which he is taking Lexapro. He had prediabetes, which he controlled by changing his diet. He has gout and muscle pain. He denies any history of thyroid issues.

Review of systems: The patient reports fatigue, weight gain, excessive appetite, bloating, shortness of breath, leg swelling, always feeling hungry, nailbed changes, dryness, itching, and muscle pain. The remainder of the review of systems is negative.

Current medications: I prescribed Coreg for hypertension, Lexapro for anxiety, and nerve pain medicine (dose and frequency not specified).

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient has bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, potentially caused by his past trauma.

Problem list: Carpal tunnel syndrome; bilateral; Carpal tunnel syndrome; confirmed.

If symptoms persist, we may consider a referral to our hand clinic for injections or carpal tunnel release surgery or refer the patient to an orthopedist for the same treatment.

Follow-up: The patient will return for a follow-up visit after the EMG test is complete.
